advisory.checklist_item: - [ ] Make SshsigScheme.verify() fail closed when ssh_keygen_version() is below SSHSIG_MIN_VERSION (not only when it is None).
evidence: src/rebar/attest/sshsig.py:107: `if ssh_keygen_version() is None:`
Module docstring/comment src/rebar/attest/sshsig.py:38-40: `OpenSSH 8.9 is the floor: it introduced valid-after/valid-before validity intervals in allowed_signers, which the trust model relies on.`
src/rebar/attest/sshsig.py:59-71: `ensure_available()` checks `version < SSHSIG_MIN_VERSION` but is never invoked from verify()
ABSENCE: no `version < SSHSIG_MIN_VERSION` check anywhere in the verify() path
advisory.finding: SshsigScheme.verify() gates only on `ssh_keygen_version() is None`, never on the SSHSIG_MIN_VERSION (8.9) floor. An OpenSSH 8.0-8.8 has `-Y verify` but lacks allowed_signers validity-interval enforcement (the module's stated reason for the 8.9 floor), so an expired `valid-before` signer would NOT be rejected on that basis — the expired-validity-window fail-closed guarantee silently does not hold on those versions.
scenarios: Verifier host running OpenSSH 8.0-8.8 verifies an attestation whose signer's allowed_signers line carries an expired `valid-before` option
advisory.suggested_fix: Replace the `is None` guard with `version = ssh_keygen_version(); if version is None or version < SSHSIG_MIN_VERSION:` returning a non-certified Verdict (or call ensure_available() and catch SshKeygenUnavailable).

advisory.checklist_item: - [ ] Detect version from the same binary that is invoked (ssh-keygen) rather than from `ssh -V`.
evidence: src/rebar/attest/sshsig.py:49: `proc = subprocess.run(["ssh", "-V"], capture_output=True, text=True)`
src/rebar/attest/sshsig.py:81: sign invokes `"ssh-keygen"`
src/rebar/attest/sshsig.py:145-158: verify invokes `"ssh-keygen"`
advisory.finding: Version detection runs `ssh -V`, but signing/verifying invoke `ssh-keygen`. On a host where `ssh` and `ssh-keygen` diverge or one is absent, verify() can misjudge availability/version — e.g. `ssh` present at 8.9 while `ssh-keygen` is missing would pass the None-check and then fail obscurely inside the subprocess call.
scenarios: Minimal container/host with `ssh` client present but `ssh-keygen` not installed, or the two binaries from different OpenSSH versions

advisory.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a fail-closed test for a principal containing a control character (e.g. "attester@rebar.test\n") asserting verified is False.
evidence: src/rebar/attest/sshsig.py:121: `if not principal or _has_control_chars(principal):`
AC: 'allowlist-validates principal + namespace (non-empty, no control chars)'
search for `control` in tests/unit found no control-char principal test for sshsig; only `test_empty_principal_fails_closed` covers the `not principal` half of the disjunction
ABSENCE: deleting `or _has_control_chars(principal)` leaves the whole suite green — regression blindness
src/rebar/attest/sshsig.py:127: `if not namespace or _has_control_chars(namespace):`
ABSENCE: both test files only pass NAMESPACE="rebar-attest" or "different-namespace" (a valid non-empty value); neither exercises empty/control-char namespace
ABSENCE: deleting the namespace guard leaves the suite green
src/rebar/attest/sshsig.py: `if not signatures:` returning verdict="mismatch", reason="no signatures to verify"
ABSENCE: `_verify` helper in test_attest_sshsig_heldout.py always builds `[dsse.Signature(...)]`; no test calls verify() with an empty signatures list
ABSENCE: removing the guard would raise IndexError on `signatures[0]` — not covered by any test
advisory.finding: The control-character principal rejection branch is untested: no test passes a principal containing a control char, so `_has_control_chars(principal)` (the AC-pinned allowlist guard against smuggling into the ssh-keygen argv) could be removed without any test failing.
merged_from: The namespace validation branch is entirely untested: no test exercises an empty or control-char namespace, so the `if not namespace or _has_control_chars(namespace)` guard has zero regression coverage despite being an AC-pinned allowlist requirement.
The empty-signatures fail-closed branch is untested: `_verify` always supplies exactly one signature, so the `if not signatures` guard returning a mismatch Verdict is never exercised.

advisory.checklist_item: - [ ] Enforce the SSHSIG_MIN_VERSION (8.9) floor inside SshsigScheme.verify() (fail closed when version < 8.9), not only the version-is-None case.
evidence: src/rebar/attest/sshsig.py:107 `if ssh_keygen_version() is None:` — only the None case fails closed; there is no `< SSHSIG_MIN_VERSION` branch in verify().
src/rebar/attest/sshsig.py:34-36 comment: 'OpenSSH 8.9 is the floor: it introduced valid-after/valid-before validity intervals in allowed_signers, which the trust model relies on.'

src/rebar/attest/sshsig.py:59-71 `ensure_available()` DOES enforce both `version is None` and `version < SSHSIG_MIN_VERSION`, but verify() neither calls ensure_available() nor duplicates the floor check.

Reachability: a host with OpenSSH 8.0-8.8 passes `ssh_keygen_version() is None` (returns a tuple, not None), reaches `ssh-keygen -Y verify`, and validity windows in trust_root are silently unenforced -> expired signer certified.
advisory.finding: SshsigScheme.verify() only fails closed when ssh_keygen_version() is None, but does NOT enforce the SSHSIG_MIN_VERSION (8.9) floor that the module documents as the basis of the validity-window trust model. On OpenSSH 8.0-8.8, `-Y verify` runs but allowed_signers valid-before/valid-after intervals are not honored, so an expired/time-revoked signer can be accepted and verify() returns a `certified` Verdict.
scenarios: Deployment running OpenSSH 8.0-8.8 verifies an attestation whose allowed_signers line carries a past valid-before; ssh-keygen ignores the interval and exits 0; verify() returns verdict='certified'.
advisory.suggested_fix: In verify(), replace `if ssh_keygen_version() is None:` with a check that fails closed when version is None OR version < SSHSIG_MIN_VERSION (e.g. call ensure_available() inside a try/except and return a non-verified Verdict on SshKeygenUnavailable).

advisory.checklist_item: - [ ] Detect the OpenSSH version from the same `ssh-keygen` binary used for verification (e.g. probe `ssh-keygen -Y` support / version) rather than from `ssh -V`.
evidence: src/rebar/attest/sshsig.py:49 `proc = subprocess.run(["ssh", "-V"], ...)` — version detected from the `ssh` client binary.
src/rebar/attest/sshsig.py:143-155 verification invokes `["ssh-keygen", "-Y", "verify", ...]` — a different binary than the one whose version was probed.

Reachability: in mixed/shimmed installs where `ssh` and `ssh-keygen` resolve to different versions or paths, ssh_keygen_version() can report an adequate version while `ssh-keygen` is absent or below 8.9.
advisory.finding: The availability/version guard probes `ssh -V`, but verification is performed with the `ssh-keygen` binary. The guard therefore validates a different binary than the one actually used to verify, so a mismatched/older `ssh-keygen` (different version or path than `ssh`) can slip past the guard.
scenarios: A host where `ssh` is 8.9+ but `ssh-keygen` on PATH is 8.0-8.8 (or vice versa) passes the version guard yet verifies with an inadequate ssh-keygen.

coaching.coaching: Extract the duplicated logic in the ssh-keygen availability/version probe binary into a shared helper.
coaching.subject: the ssh-keygen availability/version probe binary

coaching.coaching: Add a regression test covering the control-character principal rejection branch.
coaching.subject: the control-character principal rejection branch
